Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty intense but scattered. I'd dedicate about 29 hours a week to studying, but it wasn't always at the same times. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes, rewriting concepts, and creating summaries. With 16 online courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about which materials I prioritized each week. I'd often batch my studying into longer sessions when I could focus, usually late at night when my anxiety felt more manageable.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd have a solid schedule, and other weeks everything would fall apart because of competing deadlines or just feeling too scared about failing. I tried to establish regular times, but life kept getting in the way. When my stress levels spiked, I'd sometimes avoid studying altogether for a day or two, then have to cram everything in. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work somehow.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. I'd get that scared feeling in my chest and immediately log in to check what I was behind on. Sometimes I logged in just to see discussion posts from peers to feel less alone in struggling. Occasionally it was because I genuinely wanted to watch a lecture video, but honestly, fear was my biggest motivator for logging in.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it always felt like something I had to force myself to do. My attendance was pretty low because I was managing so many courses and my anxiety made it hard to show up consistently. When I did log in regularly for a stretch, it was usually because the gamified tasks made it feel slightly less daunting. But that routine never fully stuck.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most valuable to me since that matches how I learn best. I really appreciated when instructors posted detailed notes or transcripts alongside videos. The discussion forums were helpful too—seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me because they broke things into smaller, less scary pieces.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally engaged with video content, which is ironic given how much watch time I logged. I'd let videos play while doing other things because sitting through them felt overwhelming. Interactive quizzes that weren't graded I usually ignored because they felt like extra pressure I didn't need. Some of the supplementary readings I downloaded but never actually read because I was already drowning in required materials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my grades or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified elements helped because earning points or completing challenges gave me small wins when everything else felt terrifying. If something was going to take an hour but only marginally help my understanding, I couldn't justify it with everything else on my plate.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required work already had me stretched thin and anxious, so optional stuff just added to my guilt when I couldn't do it. Sometimes optional activities were actually more interesting, but the fear of falling behind on required work always won. I'd tell myself I'd come back to optional content later, but I rarely did.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, print them out, and annotate them by hand. I have notebooks filled with rewritten notes and summaries that the LMS would never know about. I also used external resources like YouTube explanations and textbooks from the library. My actual learning process was way more extensive than my online footprint suggests.
Q10: There were countless hours spent reviewing my handwritten notes, re-reading printed materials, and talking through concepts with study partners that never showed up anywhere. Sometimes I'd spend an entire weekend working through difficult concepts using my own notes and external textbooks. My peer interactions number doesn't capture the study group chats we had outside the platform. The LMS only saw a fraction of my effort.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture PDF or reading once, and then that document lived on my laptop or in printed form for weeks of studying. I'd annotate it, highlight it, come back to it multiple times—none of which registered as engagement. My assignment scores might not reflect it, but I was genuinely wrestling with that material repeatedly offline.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods when my anxiety peaked and everything felt impossible. Around midterms, I remember being so scared of failing that I almost couldn't bring myself to open the LMS at all. When multiple deadlines collided across my 16 courses, I'd shut down emotionally. My high stress levels made even simple tasks feel insurmountable, and I'd have to talk myself through every login.
Q13: Constantly. The workload from managing so many courses meant I was always triaging what absolutely had to get done versus what could slide. Fatigue was real—I'd be exhausted but unable to sleep because of anxiety about falling behind. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because facing my confusion felt too scary. My medium resources meant I couldn't always access help when I needed it most.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely—worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too anxious to absorb anything. I'd watch videos without really processing them, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was performing engagement rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew the deadline was approaching and it was making my anxiety worse. The fear of doing something wrong or not being good enough would paralyze me. I'd tell myself I needed more preparation time, but really I was just scared. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this cycle of avoidance and last-minute panic.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my anxiety and force me into action. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity right before due dates, which probably looked chaotic from the outside. Without deadlines, I'd drift and procrastinate, so the structure actually helped even though it stressed me out. The gamified tasks with their own mini-deadlines helped break this pattern a bit.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me, maybe too much. Positive feedback would boost my confidence and make me want to engage more immediately. But critical feedback would send me spiraling—I'd avoid the LMS for days afterward because I was scared to see more comments. My high resilience eventually helped me bounce back, but those initial reactions were intense.
Q18: The course structure determined a lot about when and how I participated. Courses with clear weekly modules were easier for me to follow despite my low attendance. When structure was loose, my anxiety made it hard to know if I was doing enough. The gamified interventions actually helped give me a sense of progress when the overall structure felt overwhelming.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for my learning style. Clear rubrics reduced my anxiety about assignments significantly. What made it harder was when participation was required at specific times—my schedule and mental state didn't always cooperate. Also, when there was too much content without clear priorities, I'd freeze up trying to figure out what mattered most.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking through materials without really engaging, trying to figure out what's expected but being too overwhelmed to actually produce work. Fear can drive a lot of activity that doesn't translate to output. Sometimes you're just trying to feel like you're doing something even when you can't actually do the thing.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My exam score was pretty good despite my low engagement metrics because I was doing serious work offline. The LMS doesn't see my notebooks, my study sessions with peers, or the hours I spent with printed materials. Someone can be deeply engaged with learning without that showing up in clicks and logins.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of when students are active and what resources they access. They're good at tracking completion of specific tasks and identifying who's completely absent. But they miss the quality and depth of engagement, offline studying, emotional state during sessions, and whether someone is actually learning versus just clicking through. They can't capture fear, determination, or growth.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, making connections—whether that's online or in my notebook at 2 AM. It's also about feeling connected to the course community, even when anxiety makes participation hard. True engagement is internal and can't always be measured.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I fought against my anxiety to stay in this course, or how many times I almost gave up but didn't. It can't capture the resilience it took to keep going despite being scared constantly. My peer interactions outside the platform, my extensive offline note-taking, the emotional labor of managing high stress while pursuing my education—none of that appears in the data. The Merit grade I earned represents a battle the system never witnessed.
